column5: Is this recovery safe enough to start? Are there any immediate no-go conditions? Is the scene controlled enough to proceed? Is there any reason to hold or abort before rigging begins?
column6: **Does not diagnose why the casualty is stuck, does not inventory equipment, and does not choose or build the rig.**
column7: Immediate no-go hazards have been identified and either controlled or declared unacceptable; the scene is controlled enough to proceed; the operator has made a formal **Go / Hold / Abort** decision. Continue only on **Go**.

column5: Is it traction loss, suction, belly drag, obstacle interference, slope, side-load, or mechanical bind? What resistance is preventing movement? What physical condition of vehicle, terrain, and load is causing the problem?
column6: **Does not decide whether to engage, does not assign roles, and does not choose the rig solution.**
column7: The casualty problem has been defined clearly enough to describe the recovery problem in one plain statement; the main resisting mechanisms are identified; terrain, slope/deviation, and physical extraction constraints are observed; the situation is defined well enough to move into capability matching.
column8: **ICS 201 Section 3 Map/Sketch + Section 5 Situation Summary / Health and Safety Briefing completed**

column3: **What capability do I have to rig with?**
column5: What vehicles, winches, anchors, lines, hardware, tools, and PPE do I have? What is serviceable? What is rated? What is missing? What limits do these components impose?
column6: **Does not explain why the casualty is stuck, does not justify the rig concept, and does not assign the execution plan.**
column7: Required equipment and support items have been identified; relevant gear is present, compatible, serviceable, and appropriately rated; critical deficiencies are either resolved or recognized as stopping conditions; there is enough capability for at least one valid rig option.

column5: Why direct pull, double line, redirect, bridle, anchor array, or another layout? Why this extraction direction? Why this line path? Why this level of mechanical advantage? Why is this concept better than the alternatives?
column6: **Does not inventory gear, does not assign exact people/positions/timing, and does not teach the physical setup steps.**
column7: One rig concept has been selected and justified against the baseline problem and available capability; the extraction direction, anchor concept, line path, and broad rig architecture are chosen; the concept is clear enough to sketch and confirm before detailed planning.
column8: **ICS 201 Section 2 modified diagram completed + Readiness Buddy-Checklist completed**

column5: What exact rig configuration will be used? Who does what? Where does each vehicle, anchor, block, line, damper, and person go? When do we stage, tension, test, pull, pause, and stop?
column6: **Does not debate why this concept was chosen and does not teach the handwork of actually connecting, routing, or running the rig.**
column7: The chosen concept has been converted into an exact execution plan; roles are assigned; placements are fixed; sequence, objectives, hold points, and stop triggers are defined; the plan is explicit enough to brief without improvisation.
column8: **ICS 201 Section 7 Current and Planned Objectives + Section 9 Current Organization completed**

column3: **How is this rig built and run?**
column5: How do I connect it? How do I route it? How do I position and secure each element? How do I signal, tension, spool, damp, pull, and release correctly?
column6: **Does not re-justify the rig concept, does not reassign the plan architecture, and does not diagnose why the casualty is stuck.**
column7: The rig has been physically built to plan; all connections, routing, protection, dampers, and control measures are in place; communications/signals are confirmed; pre-pull checks are passed; the system is declared ready to pull.

column5: Is the plan working? Is the vehicle moving as expected? Is the line tracking correctly? Is anything worsening, shifting, binding, or becoming unsafe? Do I continue, pause, re-rig, or abort?
column6: **Does not create the original plan, does not inventory equipment, and does not teach setup from scratch; it governs live action and sends the operator back through the loop when reality changes.**
column7: The pull has been executed and monitored; the live outcome has been assessed; a decision has been made to **continue, pause, re-rig, abort, or complete**; any material change has been recorded and, if needed, sent back into the earlier sections for revision.
